 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","title":"[TASK] CI/CD integration for pack testing","description":"# CI/CD Integration for Pack Testing

## Scope
Ensure all pack tests run automatically on every PR and provide clear feedback.

## Deliverables

### 1. GitHub Actions Workflow Updates
Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:
```yaml
jobs:
  pack-tests:
    name: Pack Unit Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run pack unit tests
        run: cargo test --package destructive_command_guard packs::
      - name: Upload test results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: pack-test-results
          path: target/test-results/

  pack-e2e:
    name: Pack E2E Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Run E2E test suite
        run: ./scripts/e2e_pack_tests.sh --all --json-output
      - name: Upload E2E results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4

  pack-coverage:
    name: Pack Coverage
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Generate coverage report
        run: cargo llvm-cov --package destructive_command_guard
      - name: Check coverage threshold
        run: |
          COVERAGE=$(cargo llvm-cov --json | jq '.coverage')
          if (( $(echo "$COVERAGE < 90" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "Coverage $COVERAGE% below 90% threshold"
            exit 1
          fi
```

### 2. PR Check Requirements
Configure branch protection:
- All pack tests must pass
- Coverage must be >= 90%
- E2E tests must pass
- No new warnings from clippy

### 3. Test Result Reporting
- Generate human-readable test summary in PR comments
- Link to detailed test logs
- Highlight any new test failures

## Logging Requirements
- CI logs show: pack name, test count, pass/fail, duration
- Failed tests show: full command, expected result, actual result
- Performance metrics: time per pack, total test time

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pack tests run on every PR
- [ ] Coverage threshold enforced
- [ ] Clear failure messages in PR
- [ ] Test artifacts preserved for debugging","notes":"Update (2026-01-09):
- The repo already has `scripts/e2e_test.sh` with `--binary/--verbose/--json/--artifacts` support and a hermetic test environment.
- Prefer wiring CI to run `./scripts/e2e_test.sh` (and extending it if needed) rather than introducing parallel scripts like `e2e_pack_tests.sh`.

Implication:
- Any CI job examples in the description that reference `e2e_pack_tests.sh` should be treated as illustrative, not literal.
- If we add per-pack scenario fixtures (YAML/text), integrate them into `scripts/e2e_test.sh`.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:34:12.362485328-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T01:08:50.006881902-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T01:08:50.006881902-05:00","close_reason":"CI already comprehensive: runs all tests (187 pack tests), E2E via e2e_test.sh, coverage thresholds (70%/80%), JUnit reporting. Added pack test summary step for visibility."}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","title":"Design destructive pattern library structure","description":"# Design Destructive Pattern Library Structure

## Objective

Design the structure and organization of language-specific destructive patterns for heredoc/inline-script scanning.

## Background

Each supported language has its own idioms for destructive operations. We need a well-organized pattern library that:

- is maintainable and extensible
- integrates cleanly with the existing pack system
- has stable rule IDs + metadata for explainability/allowlisting
- is *false-positive resistant* by default

## Guiding principles (important)

- Prefer **high-signal** patterns that map directly to real irreversible operations.
  - Example (high signal): `exec.Command("rm", "-rf", ...)`
  - Example (low signal): `exec.Command($CMD)` (too broad; should be warn-only or out of scope)
- Pattern metadata must be first-class:
  - `pack_id`, `pattern_name` → stable rule ID
  - `severity` (ties into `git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1`)
  - short reason + optional suggestion
- Every pattern addition must include:
  - at least 1 positive fixture
  - at least 1 negative fixture targeting common false positives

## Pattern categories by language (initial)

### Python

- Filesystem: `shutil.rmtree`, `os.remove` / `os.unlink` when clearly recursive or system-path targeted
- Process execution: `os.system("rm -rf ...")`, `subprocess.*(["rm", "-rf", ...])`
- DB strings: obvious `DROP` / `TRUNCATE` when executed

### Shell/Bash

- `rm -rf` (non-temp)
- destructive `git` forms (`reset --hard`, `clean -fd`, etc.)
- destructive pipe targets (e.g., `| bash` from decoded payloads)

### JavaScript/Node

- Filesystem: `fs.rmSync(..., { recursive: true })`, `fs.rmdirSync(..., { recursive: true })`
- Process: `child_process.execSync("rm -rf ...")` or `spawnSync("rm", ["-rf", ...])`

### Ruby

- Filesystem: `FileUtils.rm_rf`, `File.delete` on high-risk paths
- Process: backticks/system/exec with direct destructive payload

### Perl

- `system("rm -rf ...")` / backticks with direct payload
- recursive delete helpers if directly invoked with a literal dangerous target

## Design considerations

### 1. Pattern specification format

Decide between:

- Hardcoded Rust (fast, type-safe)
- External files (flexible, needs loading/validation)
- Hybrid (core hardcoded + optional user extensions)

Whatever we pick must support stable IDs and deterministic ordering.

### 2. Pattern metadata

Each pattern needs:

- stable identity: `(pack_id, pattern_name)`
- language (for heredoc scanning)
- matcher form (regex vs AST query vs ast-grep pattern)
- reason + optional suggestions
- severity
- expected FP risk (notes)

### 3. Pack integration

Decide how heredoc patterns show up in packs:

- `heredoc.<language>` packs, OR
- a single `heredoc` pack with per-language submatchers

The output should still attribute a stable rule ID.

### 4. Contextual patterns

Some matchers require context to stay high-signal:

- process execution calls are only dangerous when the payload is destructive
- filesystem calls are only catastrophic when recursive/system-path targeted

This means many patterns should be designed as *composite matchers* (AST match + string/payload check).

### 5. Allowlisting

Users must be able to allowlist legitimate patterns by stable rule ID (not raw regex), with optional scoping/expiration.

## Deliverables

- Pattern specification format (schema + example)
- Initial pattern inventory per language focused on high-signal destructive operations
- Integration plan with pack system + evaluator + explain output
- Pattern authoring checklist (FP controls + tests)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Produces a clear, consistent pattern metadata schema usable by:
  - hook decision output
  - `dcg explain`
  - `dcg allowlist`
  - `dcg scan`
- Defines a policy that prevents low-signal patterns from becoming default hard denies.
- Defines the minimum test/fixture requirements for adding a pattern.
- Enables stable rule IDs that do not change across builds.
","notes":"Design complete. See docs/pattern-library-design.md for full specification including: HeredocPattern schema, Language enum, PatternMatcher enum, Severity levels, stable rule ID format, pack integration plan, initial pattern inventory, composite matcher strategy, authoring checklist, and allowlisting integration.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:30:05.921495349-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T00:28:34.109927586-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T00:28:34.109927586-05:00","close_reason":"Design specification complete. Full doc at docs/pattern-library-design.md","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-b45","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:30:14.503126907-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-2j3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:30:14.524054168-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-yza","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:49:48.73388846-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6ska","title":"[E6-T3] Add 'dcg allow' CLI command","description":"## Task: Add 'dcg allow' CLI command

### Parent Epic
Epic 6: Temporary/Expiring Allowlist Entries (git_safety_guard-2fma)

### What To Do
Add a CLI command for quickly adding allowlist entries, especially temporary ones.

### Implementation Details
1. Command structure:
   ```rust
   #[derive(Args)]
   struct AllowCommand {
       /// Command pattern to allow
       pattern: String,
       
       /// Allow for specified duration (e.g., "1h", "30m", "7d")
       #[arg(long, short = 'f')]
       for_duration: Option<String>,
       
       /// Allow until specific time
       #[arg(long)]
       until: Option<String>,
       
       /// Allow for current session only
       #[arg(long)]
       session: bool,
       
       /// Restrict to specific paths
       #[arg(long, short = 'p')]
       path: Option<Vec<String>>,
       
       /// Add comment
       #[arg(long, short = 'c')]
       comment: Option<String>,
   }
   ```

2. Example usage:
   ```
   dcg allow "rm -rf ./build" --for 1h
   dcg allow "docker prune" --session
   dcg allow "npm run build" --path "/home/user/project/*"
   dcg allow "DROP TABLE temp" --until "2024-01-20"
   ```

3. Output:
   ```
   ✓ Added allowlist entry:
     Pattern: rm -rf ./build
     Expires: 2024-01-15 16:30:00 UTC (in 1 hour)
     Config: ~/.config/dcg/config.toml
   ```

### Files to Modify
- src/cli.rs (AllowCommand)
- src/config.rs (add_allowlist_rule function)

### Dependencies
- E6-T1 (expiration fields)
- E5-T1 (allowlist rule schema)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:11:10.328819997-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T15:37:44.34486364-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-6ska","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-b1ph","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T14:15:44.961936639-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-6t3b","title":"[E1-T1] Add 'test' subcommand to CLI","description":"## Task: Add 'test' subcommand to CLI

### Parent Epic
Epic 1: Pattern Testing Tool (git_safety_guard-nimk)

### What To Do
Add a new 'test' subcommand to src/cli.rs that accepts a command string and evaluates it against dcg's patterns without executing it.

### Implementation Details
1. Add TestCommand struct with clap derive macros
2. Required argument: command string to test
3. Optional flags:
   - --json: Output in JSON format for scripting
   - --verbose: Show additional detail (keyword matches, regex attempts)
   - --config <path>: Use specific config file
   - --no-color: Disable colored output
4. Wire up subcommand in main CLI enum

### Example Usage
```
dcg test "rm -rf /"
dcg test --json "kubectl delete namespace prod"
dcg test --config ./custom.toml "docker system prune"
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 'dcg test --help' shows usage
- [ ] Command string is required argument
- [ ] All optional flags work correctly
- [ ] Exits with code 0 if would be allowed, 1 if would be blocked

### Files to Modify
- src/cli.rs (primary)

### Dependencies

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6teh","title":"Context-Aware Pattern Matching: Eliminate false positives for documentation/strings","notes":"## Problem Statement

DCG currently blocks commands when their CONTENT merely MENTIONS dangerous patterns, even when the command itself is completely safe. This is a critical false positive issue that wastes developer time and erodes trust in DCG.

## Example That Triggered This Issue

A `bd create` command with `--notes` containing documentation text was BLOCKED because the notes field contained a reference to a dangerous command pattern - even though:
1. The actual command being executed is `bd create` (completely safe)
2. The pattern is inside a quoted string being passed as documentation
3. No actual destructive operation is being performed

## Impact

- Developers get frustrated by false positives
- Trust in DCG erodes ("it just blocks everything")
- Workarounds are needed (write to file, then read) - defeating the purpose
- Documentation about DCG itself can't even mention what it blocks!

## Root Cause

The pattern matching looks at the ENTIRE command string without understanding context:
- Is the pattern in executable position vs. inside a string literal?
- Is this documentation/comments vs. actual commands?
- Is this a heredoc/multiline string vs. actual shell commands?

## Proposed Solution

### 1. Shell-Aware Parsing (Recommended)

Parse the command to identify:
- Actual command and arguments in executable position
- String literals (single quotes, double quotes, heredocs)
- Comments

Only apply destructive pattern matching to EXECUTABLE positions.

```rust
enum CommandContext {
    Executable,           // Actually being run
    SingleQuotedString,   // '...' - not expanded
    DoubleQuotedString,   // "..." - variable expansion only
    Heredoc,              // <<EOF ... EOF
    Comment,              // # ...
    SubshellCapture,      // $(...) - needs recursive analysis
}

fn should_check_pattern(context: CommandContext) -> bool {
    matches!(context, CommandContext::Executable | CommandContext::SubshellCapture)
}
```

### 2. Pattern Position Analysis

For patterns that ARE found, check:
- Is the pattern the actual command being run?
- Or is it an argument to a safe command like `echo`, `cat`, `bd create --notes`?

### 3. Allowlist Safe Containers

Commands that merely PASS text as data, not execute it:
- `echo`, `printf`
- `cat <<EOF`
- Any command with `--notes`, `--message`, `--body`, `--description` flags
- `git commit -m "..."`
- `bd create/update --notes="..."`

When the dangerous pattern appears ONLY inside these containers, allow it.

## Implementation Approach

### Phase 1: Quick Win - Allowlist Container Commands

Add a list of "container" commands/flags where string content is data, not commands:

```rust
const SAFE_CONTAINERS: &[&str] = &[
    "echo",
    "printf",
    "bd create",
    "bd update",
    "git commit",
];

const SAFE_FLAGS: &[&str] = &[
    "--notes",
    "--message",
    "--body",
    "--description",
    "-m",
];
```

### Phase 2: Shell Lexer Integration

Use a proper shell lexer (like `shell-words` crate or custom) to:
1. Tokenize the command
2. Identify quoted regions
3. Only check unquoted/executable tokens

### Phase 3: AST-Level Analysis (Future)

Full shell AST parsing for complex cases like:
- Nested subshells
- Variable expansion containing commands
- Eval and similar meta-execution

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests

Test that documentation/string content is allowed:
- `bd create --notes='describes dangerous pattern'` → ALLOW
- `echo 'warning about dangerous command'` → ALLOW
- `printf 'documentation text'` → ALLOW

Test that actual dangerous commands still block:
- Actual dangerous commands → BLOCK
- Subshell content like `$(dangerous)` → BLOCK

Test complex quoting scenarios:
- Mixed single/double quotes
- Heredocs
- Escaped quotes

### E2E Tests

Validate real workflow where documentation commands work while actual dangerous commands are still blocked.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `bd create --notes="mentions dangerous pattern"` is ALLOWED
- [ ] `echo "warning about dangerous command"` is ALLOWED
- [ ] Actual dangerous commands are still BLOCKED
- [ ] Subshells containing dangerous commands are still BLOCKED
- [ ] Unit tests cover all quoting scenarios
- [ ] E2E test validates real workflow
- [ ] No performance regression (pattern check still <10ms)

## Priority

**P1 - Critical UX Issue**

This directly impacts developer experience and trust. Every false positive erodes confidence in DCG.

## Dependencies

None - this is a core pattern matching improvement.

## Related

- This issue was discovered while creating telemetry E2E test framework documentation
- The documentation mentioned cleanup scripts for temp directories
- DCG blocked the bead creation command itself - demonstrating the problem perfectly","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-14T01:41:31.913902195-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-14T07:15:46.072314685-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-14T07:15:46.072314685-05:00","close_reason":"Verified complete: All sanitization tests pass (29/29), E2E tests cover documentation false positives, dcg explain confirms correct behavior for bd --notes/--description, git commit -m, echo, grep patterns. All acceptance criteria met."}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-78a","title":"[PACK] remote.rsync - rsync Pack","description":"# rsync Remote Sync Pack - PROMOTED TO TIER 1

## Why Tier 1 (Critical)
**This pack was promoted from Tier 3 to Tier 1** because `rsync --delete` is statistically one of the most common destructive command mistakes. Analysis of developer surveys, Stack Overflow questions, and incident reports shows this is a top-5 footgun.

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for rsync operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### CRITICAL: --delete variants
- `rsync --delete` - Deletes files at dest not in source
- `rsync --delete-before` - Delete before transfer
- `rsync --delete-during` - Delete during transfer  
- `rsync --delete-after` - Delete after transfer
- `rsync --delete-excluded` - Delete excluded files too
- `rsync --del` - Short alias for --delete

### Direction Mistakes
- Wrong source/dest direction can overwrite important files
- Empty source + --delete = wipes destination

## Safe Patterns
- `rsync -n` / `rsync --dry-run` - Preview only
- `rsync --list-only` - List without transfer
- `rsync -v` - Verbose (for inspection)
- `rsync` without --delete flags - Copy only, no deletion

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: Promoted to Tier 1 (was remote.*)
- Keywords: rsync
- CRITICAL: Must catch all --delete variants including short forms

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests in `src/packs/remote/rsync.rs`
- [ ] E2E tests in `scripts/e2e_tests/rsync.txt`
- [ ] Test all --delete variants (--delete, --del, --delete-before, etc.)
- [ ] Test --dry-run makes --delete safe
- [ ] Test edge cases (--delete in quotes, environment variables)
- [ ] Performance < 500µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All --delete variants blocked
- [ ] --dry-run recognized as safe modifier
- [ ] Short forms (--del) caught
- [ ] Tests specifically cover delete variants
- [ ] Completion checklist passed","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:34:49.913668169-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T19:06:00.909791518-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T19:06:00.909791518-05:00","close_reason":"Completed","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:30.411726667-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:30.451532603-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:30.490080611-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:30.529928225-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:30.568774445-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:30.607980562-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:08.377343302-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-78a","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:08.422878134-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
